questions.prompt: What causes the output of a MOSFET inverter to not switch instantaneously?
options: The threshold voltage is too high
The parasitic capacitance Co must be charged/discharged
The supply voltage VDD is limited
questions.correctAnswer: The parasitic capacitance Co must be charged/discharged
questions.explanation: The parasitic capacitance Co at the output node stores charge. To change the output voltage, this charge must be added (charging) or removed (discharging) through the transistors' ON resistances. This takes time, creating the switching delay. The larger Co is, the slower the transition.

questions.prompt: Which time constant governs the falling edge (output going LOW) in an active-load inverter?
questions.explanation: The falling edge occurs when T1 (the driver) turns ON and discharges Co through its ON resistance. So the time constant is τ₁ = Co × Rsat(T1). T2 (the load) controls the rising edge.

questions.prompt: Why is the rising edge of an NMOS active-load inverter slower than the falling edge?
options: T1 has a higher threshold voltage
T2 has a higher ON resistance than T1
The supply voltage drops during charging
The capacitor is larger during charging
questions.correctAnswer: T2 has a higher ON resistance than T1
questions.explanation: The rising edge is governed by τ₂ = Co × Rsat(T2). Since T2 is a diode-connected load with VGS2 = VDS2, it has a higher ON resistance than T1 (which is driven hard by the full gate voltage). Higher resistance means slower RC charging.

questions.prompt: What is the formula for the ON resistance of a MOSFET?
questions.explanation: The ON resistance is defined as the ratio of the drain-source voltage when ON to the drain current: Rsat = VDS(ON)/IDS. This is essentially Ohm's law applied to the transistor in its low-resistance state.

questions.prompt: If Ro = 1kΩ, Rsat = 4kΩ, and VDD = 15V, what is the steady-state output LOW voltage?
questions.explanation: Using the voltage divider formula: Vo1 = (Ro/(Ro + Rsat)) × VDD = (1k/(1k + 4k)) × 15V = (1/5) × 15V = 3V. The output doesn't reach 0V because of the voltage divider between Ro and the load's ON resistance.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of a pull-up resistor in an open-drain output circuit?
options: To pull the output HIGH when the transistor is OFF
To protect the transistor from overvoltage
questions.correctAnswer: To pull the output HIGH when the transistor is OFF
questions.explanation: An open-drain output can only pull LOW (when the transistor is ON). When the transistor is OFF, there's no internal path to pull the output HIGH. The external pull-up resistor provides this path, connecting the output to +VDD so it rises to logic HIGH when the transistor is OFF.

questions.prompt: Why can multiple open-drain outputs share a common data bus without conflict?
options: They can only pull LOW — there's no push-pull conflict
questions.correctAnswer: They can only pull LOW — there's no push-pull conflict
questions.explanation: Open-drain outputs can only actively drive the bus LOW. When OFF, they present high impedance. So if two outputs try to pull LOW simultaneously, both simply conduct — no conflict. The conflict only occurs with push-pull outputs where one drives HIGH while another drives LOW, creating a short circuit.

questions.prompt: What is the technique called when multiple circuits take turns placing data on a shared bus?
questions.explanation: When multiple open-drain circuits share a bus, they take turns driving the bus at different time intervals. This is called time division multiplexing (TDM). Each circuit gets a time slot to put its data on the bus, while all others keep their transistors OFF.

questions.prompt: If Co = 15pF and Rsat(T1) = 200Ω, what is the falling edge time constant?
questions.explanation: τ₁ = Co × Rsat(T1) = 15 × 10⁻¹² F × 200 Ω = 3 × 10⁻⁹ s = 3 ns. This is the time constant for the exponential decay of the output voltage during the falling edge.

questions.prompt: What happens if you forget the external pull-up resistor on an open-drain output?
options: The output floats when the transistor is OFF, causing unpredictable behaviour
questions.correctAnswer: The output floats when the transistor is OFF, causing unpredictable behaviour
questions.explanation: Without a pull-up resistor, when the transistor is OFF there is no path to pull the output to any defined voltage. It becomes a floating node that can pick up noise and hover at indeterminate voltages, causing unreliable logic operation in downstream circuits.
